Case Studies: Context-Dependent miR Behavior Across Cancer Types

Amir (Building on Kevin R. Coombes’ framework)

Original: 2026-01-05; Latest Run: 2026-01-05

  • Summary
  • Getting Started
  • Utility Functions
  • Case Study 1: let-7a - The Canonical Tumor Suppressor
    • Known let-7a Targets
  • Case Study 2: miR-34a - The p53 Effector
    • Known miR-34a Targets
  • Case Study 3: Deep Dive into miR-362 (Kevin’s Example)
    • Genes with Opposite Behavior
  • Clustering Cancer Types by miR Correlation Patterns
  • Summary of Findings
  • Appendix

Summary

What we learned from the pathway analysis (401): In our previous analysis, we discovered that context-dependency is the rule, not the exception. For well-studied miRNAs like let-7a, miR-34a, and miR-362, essentially 100% of genes show context-dependent (mixed positive/negative) correlation across cancer types. Even for miR-21 (the most studied oncomiR), only 14 out of 20,193 genes (0.07%) show consistently negative correlation.

Goal of this analysis: Dive deeper into specific case studies to understand why certain miR-gene pairs behave differently across cancer types. We will:

  1. Examine let-7a (tumor suppressor) - which cancers show strongest negative correlation?
  2. Study miR-34a (p53-regulated) - does it behave as a tumor suppressor uniformly?
  3. Extend Kevin’s miR-362 example with additional functional analysis
  4. Identify cancer types that cluster by miR correlation patterns

Key Questions: - Are there cancer-specific miR “signatures” in correlation patterns? - Do known oncogenic contexts show weaker tumor suppressor correlations? - Can we identify tissue-specific miR targets?

Getting Started

source("00-paths.R")

library(ggplot2)
library(pheatmap)
library(RColorBrewer)
load(file.path(paths$mirtcga, "matchedNoGBM.rda"))
load(file.path(paths$mirtcga, "cohortColors.Rda"))

cat("Data loaded:\n")
## Data loaded:
cat("  Genes:", nrow(mRNAdata), "\n")
##   Genes: 20289
cat("  miRs:", nrow(miRdata), "\n")
##   miRs: 1146
cat("  Samples:", ncol(mRNAdata), "\n")
##   Samples: 8890
cat("  Cohorts:", paste(sort(unique(cancerType)), collapse=", "), "\n")
##   Cohorts: ACC, BLCA, BRCA, CESC, CHOL, COAD, DLBC, ESCA, HNSC, KICH, KIRC, KIRP, LGG, LIHC, LUAD, LUSC, MESO, OV, PAAD, PCPG, PRAD, READ, SARC, SKCM, STAD, TGCT, THCA, THYM, UCEC, UCS, UVM

Utility Functions

I’ll use the same correlation approach as before, but add functions for visualization and comparison across cohorts.

# Compute correlation for one miR with all genes in one cohort
compute_cohort_cor <- function(mir_name, cohort, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType) {
  idx <- cancerType == cohort
  if (sum(idx) < 10) return(rep(NA, nrow(mRNAdata)))

  mir_expr <- as.numeric(miRdata[mir_name, idx])
  if (all(mir_expr == 0) || sd(mir_expr) == 0) return(rep(NA, nrow(mRNAdata)))

  cors <- sapply(1:nrow(mRNAdata), function(i) {
    mrna_expr <- as.numeric(mRNAdata[i, idx])
    if (sd(mrna_expr) > 0) {
      cor(mir_expr, mrna_expr, use = "complete.obs")
    } else {
      NA
    }
  })
  names(cors) <- rownames(mRNAdata)
  return(cors)
}

# Compute correlation matrix for one miR across all cohorts
compute_mir_matrix <- function(mir_name, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType) {
  cohorts <- sort(unique(as.character(cancerType)))
  result <- matrix(NA, nrow = nrow(mRNAdata), ncol = length(cohorts),
                   dimnames = list(rownames(mRNAdata), cohorts))

  for (ct in cohorts) {
    result[, ct] <- compute_cohort_cor(mir_name, ct, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType)
  }
  return(result)
}

Case Study 1: let-7a - The Canonical Tumor Suppressor

Let-7a was one of the first discovered miRNAs and is well-established as a tumor suppressor. It directly targets oncogenes like RAS and HMGA2. If correlation reflects regulation, we should see consistent negative correlation with these targets.

# Find let-7a variants
let7a_mirs <- grep("let-7a", rownames(miRdata), value = TRUE)
cat("Available let-7a variants:\n")
## Available let-7a variants:
print(let7a_mirs)
## [1] "hsa-let-7a-2-3p" "hsa-let-7a-3p"   "hsa-let-7a-5p"
# Use the 5p form (more commonly studied)
let7a <- grep("let-7a-5p", rownames(miRdata), value = TRUE)[1]
cat("\nUsing:", let7a, "\n")
## 
## Using: hsa-let-7a-5p
cat("Computing correlations for", let7a, "across all cohorts...\n")
## Computing correlations for hsa-let-7a-5p across all cohorts...
let7a_cors <- compute_mir_matrix(let7a, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType)
cat("Done!\n")
## Done!

Let me first look at the distribution of correlations across all cohorts.

# Calculate summary statistics per cohort
let7a_summary <- data.frame(
  cohort = colnames(let7a_cors),
  mean_cor = apply(let7a_cors, 2, mean, na.rm = TRUE),
  median_cor = apply(let7a_cors, 2, median, na.rm = TRUE),
  n_negative = apply(let7a_cors, 2, function(x) sum(x < -0.3, na.rm = TRUE)),
  n_positive = apply(let7a_cors, 2, function(x) sum(x > 0.3, na.rm = TRUE))
)

# Which cohorts have the most negative correlations?
cat("Cohorts ranked by genes with strong negative correlation (r < -0.3):\n")
## Cohorts ranked by genes with strong negative correlation (r < -0.3):
print(let7a_summary[order(-let7a_summary$n_negative), c("cohort", "n_negative", "n_positive")])
##      cohort n_negative n_positive
## UVM     UVM       2276        806
## THYM   THYM       1929       2782
## PRAD   PRAD       1758        427
## MESO   MESO       1287       1622
## DLBC   DLBC       1194       1298
## LUAD   LUAD        925        538
## UCS     UCS        820        500
## TGCT   TGCT        801       2528
## BLCA   BLCA        542          7
## CHOL   CHOL        496        732
## LUSC   LUSC        488        687
## SARC   SARC        417        238
## HNSC   HNSC        286        285
## COAD   COAD        171          9
## OV       OV        131        140
## READ   READ        114        422
## THCA   THCA         89        174
## UCEC   UCEC         87         84
## ACC     ACC         72         42
## LIHC   LIHC         68         46
## PCPG   PCPG         64         32
## KICH   KICH         50        172
## BRCA   BRCA         47         64
## STAD   STAD         28         62
## SKCM   SKCM         12          4
## KIRP   KIRP          8          9
## CESC   CESC          3          8
## PAAD   PAAD          3         12
## ESCA   ESCA          2          8
## KIRC   KIRC          1          0
## LGG     LGG          0          1

This is fascinating! Let’s visualize which cohorts show the strongest tumor suppressor-like behavior (negative correlations).

# Melt for plotting
let7a_plot <- let7a_summary[order(let7a_summary$n_negative), ]
let7a_plot$cohort <- factor(let7a_plot$cohort, levels = let7a_plot$cohort)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
barplot(let7a_plot$n_negative, names.arg = let7a_plot$cohort, las = 2,
        col = cohortColors[as.character(let7a_plot$cohort)],
        main = "Genes with r < -0.3 (let-7a)", ylab = "Number of genes")

barplot(let7a_plot$n_positive, names.arg = let7a_plot$cohort, las = 2,
        col = cohortColors[as.character(let7a_plot$cohort)],
        main = "Genes with r > 0.3 (let-7a)", ylab = "Number of genes")

Genes with strong let-7a correlation by cancer type.

Known let-7a Targets

Let’s check if known oncogenic targets of let-7a show negative correlation. Classic let-7a targets include RAS genes, HMGA2, and MYC.

# Known let-7a targets
known_targets <- c("KRAS", "NRAS", "HRAS", "HMGA2", "MYC", "LIN28A", "LIN28B", "IGF2BP1", "IGF2BP2")

# Check which are in our data
available_targets <- known_targets[known_targets %in% rownames(let7a_cors)]
cat("Available known targets:", paste(available_targets, collapse = ", "), "\n\n")
## Available known targets: KRAS, NRAS, HRAS, HMGA2, MYC, LIN28A, LIN28B, IGF2BP1, IGF2BP2
# Get their correlations across cohorts
if (length(available_targets) > 0) {
  target_cors <- let7a_cors[available_targets, , drop = FALSE]

  cat("Correlation of known let-7a targets across cohorts:\n")
  print(round(target_cors, 3))

  cat("\nSummary: proportion of cohorts with negative correlation:\n")
  prop_neg <- apply(target_cors, 1, function(x) mean(x < 0, na.rm = TRUE))
  print(round(prop_neg, 2))
}
## Correlation of known let-7a targets across cohorts:
##            ACC   BLCA   BRCA   CESC   CHOL   COAD   DLBC   ESCA   HNSC   KICH   KIRC   KIRP
## KRAS    -0.025  0.006 -0.050  0.013 -0.133  0.150 -0.045 -0.047  0.068  0.090  0.081  0.069
## NRAS     0.014  0.026 -0.069 -0.079 -0.393  0.034 -0.293 -0.056  0.150  0.089  0.079  0.174
## HRAS     0.010  0.059 -0.100  0.015  0.112 -0.107  0.027  0.012 -0.239 -0.103 -0.011 -0.092
## HMGA2   -0.010 -0.074 -0.091 -0.275 -0.084 -0.149 -0.292 -0.140 -0.193  0.062 -0.076 -0.069
## MYC      0.154 -0.056  0.076 -0.052  0.033  0.021  0.172  0.048  0.033  0.084  0.104 -0.141
## LIN28A   0.083 -0.003 -0.136 -0.306 -0.112 -0.021 -0.239  0.159 -0.020  0.156 -0.031  0.036
## LIN28B  -0.182 -0.004 -0.133 -0.087 -0.065  0.032 -0.502  0.028 -0.122 -0.088  0.009 -0.131
## IGF2BP1 -0.102 -0.060 -0.176 -0.183 -0.282 -0.104 -0.113  0.032 -0.093  0.065 -0.165 -0.025
## IGF2BP2 -0.026 -0.055 -0.121 -0.230 -0.199 -0.102 -0.243 -0.049 -0.151 -0.041 -0.217 -0.136
##            LGG   LIHC   LUAD   LUSC   MESO     OV   PAAD   PCPG   PRAD   READ   SARC   SKCM
## KRAS     0.006  0.198 -0.123 -0.137 -0.196 -0.150  0.049 -0.043  0.060  0.124  0.049 -0.243
## NRAS    -0.020 -0.058  0.055  0.009 -0.344 -0.223  0.056  0.072  0.047  0.003  0.256 -0.234
## HRAS    -0.092 -0.294 -0.194 -0.245  0.350 -0.025 -0.012 -0.115  0.111 -0.218  0.106  0.156
## HMGA2    0.006 -0.111 -0.099 -0.256  0.001 -0.257  0.065  0.074 -0.106 -0.107  0.217 -0.251
## MYC     -0.089  0.014  0.107 -0.140 -0.155  0.169  0.103  0.191  0.169  0.092  0.236 -0.132
## LIN28A  -0.002 -0.145 -0.196 -0.175 -0.016 -0.161 -0.110 -0.154 -0.052 -0.036 -0.066 -0.317
## LIN28B  -0.030 -0.375 -0.197 -0.152  0.074 -0.380 -0.049 -0.121  0.050  0.212 -0.075 -0.404
## IGF2BP1 -0.031 -0.212 -0.226 -0.180 -0.029 -0.296  0.065 -0.066  0.017 -0.018  0.002 -0.250
## IGF2BP2  0.037 -0.109 -0.125 -0.210 -0.279 -0.198  0.090  0.083 -0.307 -0.262  0.143 -0.123
##           STAD   TGCT   THCA   THYM   UCEC    UCS    UVM
## KRAS     0.137 -0.309 -0.006 -0.242 -0.174 -0.104 -0.268
## NRAS     0.161 -0.226 -0.001 -0.187 -0.101 -0.301 -0.262
## HRAS    -0.135 -0.119 -0.199 -0.045  0.146  0.092  0.231
## HMGA2   -0.030 -0.024 -0.089  0.236 -0.243 -0.433 -0.069
## MYC     -0.037  0.235  0.023 -0.334 -0.147  0.089 -0.067
## LIN28A   0.043 -0.317  0.031  0.049 -0.203  0.097 -0.202
## LIN28B  -0.121 -0.329 -0.038 -0.141 -0.103 -0.579 -0.340
## IGF2BP1 -0.026 -0.339 -0.013  0.274 -0.314 -0.381 -0.002
## IGF2BP2  0.059 -0.259  0.038  0.464 -0.408 -0.293 -0.160
## 
## Summary: proportion of cohorts with negative correlation:
##    KRAS    NRAS    HRAS   HMGA2     MYC  LIN28A  LIN28B IGF2BP1 IGF2BP2 
##    0.55    0.52    0.58    0.77    0.35    0.74    0.81    0.81    0.77
if (length(available_targets) > 2) {
  # Remove NA rows/cols
  target_cors_clean <- target_cors[, colSums(!is.na(target_cors)) > 0]

  pheatmap(target_cors_clean,
           main = "let-7a correlation with known targets",
           color = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(100),
           breaks = seq(-1, 1, length.out = 101),
           cluster_rows = TRUE,
           cluster_cols = TRUE,
           fontsize_row = 10,
           fontsize_col = 8)
}

Correlation of known let-7a targets across cancer types.

Interesting! Even for known direct targets of let-7a, the correlation patterns are highly variable across cancer types. This strongly supports Kevin’s thesis that context matters enormously.

Case Study 2: miR-34a - The p53 Effector

miR-34a is transcriptionally activated by p53 and mediates tumor suppression. Its targets include CDK6, E2F3, and BCL2. Since p53 status varies across cancers, we might expect miR-34a to behave differently depending on p53 functionality.

mir34a <- grep("miR-34a-5p", rownames(miRdata), value = TRUE)[1]
cat("Analyzing:", mir34a, "\n")
## Analyzing: hsa-miR-34a-5p
cat("Computing correlations...\n")
## Computing correlations...
mir34a_cors <- compute_mir_matrix(mir34a, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType)
cat("Done!\n")
## Done!
# Summary statistics
mir34a_summary <- data.frame(
  cohort = colnames(mir34a_cors),
  mean_cor = apply(mir34a_cors, 2, mean, na.rm = TRUE),
  n_strong_neg = apply(mir34a_cors, 2, function(x) sum(x < -0.3, na.rm = TRUE)),
  n_strong_pos = apply(mir34a_cors, 2, function(x) sum(x > 0.3, na.rm = TRUE))
)

cat("miR-34a: Cohorts with most strongly correlated genes:\n")
## miR-34a: Cohorts with most strongly correlated genes:
print(mir34a_summary[order(-mir34a_summary$n_strong_neg), ])
##      cohort      mean_cor n_strong_neg n_strong_pos
## THYM   THYM  0.0271897020         2826         3443
## THCA   THCA -0.0408115872         2386         1213
## STAD   STAD -0.0370009567         2035          731
## TGCT   TGCT -0.0161565975         1937         1792
## KIRC   KIRC -0.0038430999         1932         1654
## LGG     LGG  0.0229340085         1772         2279
## KIRP   KIRP -0.0395587723         1735          942
## SARC   SARC -0.0105478748         1612         1284
## BLCA   BLCA -0.0170227694         1175          647
## PRAD   PRAD  0.0071358135         1045          931
## READ   READ -0.0326280492          804          237
## PCPG   PCPG  0.0059940557          779          841
## UVM     UVM  0.0194685530          751         1037
## DLBC   DLBC  0.0129944629          702          821
## UCEC   UCEC -0.0187692232          672          242
## CHOL   CHOL  0.0027172204          644          714
## UCS     UCS  0.0114429550          517          905
## ACC     ACC  0.0024921822          512          534
## KICH   KICH -0.0069197804          484          202
## PAAD   PAAD -0.0319458021          423          240
## COAD   COAD -0.0341460321          390           74
## HNSC   HNSC  0.0349825785          377          694
## ESCA   ESCA  0.0015201740          368          419
## MESO   MESO -0.0206463243          303          155
## LUAD   LUAD  0.0132261401          179          565
## LIHC   LIHC -0.0122627717          175          210
## OV       OV  0.0082141466           43          143
## CESC   CESC  0.0002760474           28           12
## LUSC   LUSC -0.0006027795           23           26
## SKCM   SKCM -0.0067244032            1           22
## BRCA   BRCA -0.0061704424            0            0

Known miR-34a Targets

# Known miR-34a targets (cell cycle, apoptosis)
mir34a_known <- c("CDK6", "CDK4", "E2F3", "BCL2", "NOTCH1", "MET", "SIRT1", "MYC")
available_34a <- mir34a_known[mir34a_known %in% rownames(mir34a_cors)]

cat("Checking known miR-34a targets:\n")
## Checking known miR-34a targets:
if (length(available_34a) > 0) {
  target34_cors <- mir34a_cors[available_34a, , drop = FALSE]
  print(round(target34_cors, 3))

  cat("\nProportion of cohorts with negative correlation:\n")
  print(round(apply(target34_cors, 1, function(x) mean(x < 0, na.rm = TRUE)), 2))
}
##           ACC   BLCA   BRCA   CESC   CHOL   COAD   DLBC   ESCA   HNSC   KICH   KIRC   KIRP
## CDK6   -0.333 -0.322 -0.001 -0.180  0.008 -0.049 -0.137 -0.006 -0.318  0.050 -0.049 -0.099
## CDK4    0.072 -0.004 -0.009 -0.040  0.276 -0.094 -0.313  0.026  0.221 -0.253  0.207  0.071

## MET     0.467  0.027  0.127  0.032  0.074  0.084  0.111 -0.116 -0.091  0.183  0.158  0.099

Case Study 3: Deep Dive into miR-362 (Kevin’s Example)

Kevin showed that miR-362 has Cohen’s kappa of 0.120 in PRAD but only 0.003 in SKCM for association with validated targets. Let me explore this more.

mir362 <- grep("miR-362-3p", rownames(miRdata), value = TRUE)[1]
cat("Analyzing:", mir362, "\n")
## Analyzing: hsa-miR-362-3p
mir362_cors <- compute_mir_matrix(mir362, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType)
# Compare PRAD and SKCM directly
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# PRAD
hist(mir362_cors[, "PRAD"], breaks = 50, main = "miR-362 in PRAD",
     xlab = "Correlation", col = cohortColors["PRAD"])
abline(v = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

# SKCM
hist(mir362_cors[, "SKCM"], breaks = 50, main = "miR-362 in SKCM",
     xlab = "Correlation", col = cohortColors["SKCM"])
abline(v = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

miR-362 correlation patterns in PRAD vs SKCM. 

# Scatter plot comparing the two
plot(mir362_cors[, "PRAD"], mir362_cors[, "SKCM"],
     pch = 16, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.1), cex = 0.5,
     xlab = "Correlation in PRAD", ylab = "Correlation in SKCM",
     main = "miR-362: PRAD vs SKCM (each point = 1 gene)")
abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2)
abline(h = 0, v = 0, col = "gray", lty = 2)

# Correlation between the correlation profiles
rho <- cor(mir362_cors[, "PRAD"], mir362_cors[, "SKCM"], use = "complete.obs")
legend("topleft", legend = paste("r =", round(rho, 3)), bty = "n", cex = 1.2)

Comparing miR-362 correlations: PRAD vs SKCM.

The correlation between PRAD and SKCM correlation profiles is only -0.014! This means the same miR has essentially unrelated correlation patterns in these two cancer types.

Genes with Opposite Behavior

Let’s identify genes where miR-362 shows opposite correlation in PRAD vs SKCM.

# Find genes that flip sign
prad_neg_skcm_pos <- which(mir362_cors[, "PRAD"] < -0.2 & mir362_cors[, "SKCM"] > 0.2)
prad_pos_skcm_neg <- which(mir362_cors[, "PRAD"] > 0.2 & mir362_cors[, "SKCM"] < -0.2)

cat("Genes with opposite correlation patterns:\n")
## Genes with opposite correlation patterns:
cat("  Negative in PRAD, Positive in SKCM:", length(prad_neg_skcm_pos), "\n")
##   Negative in PRAD, Positive in SKCM: 16
cat("  Positive in PRAD, Negative in SKCM:", length(prad_pos_skcm_neg), "\n")
##   Positive in PRAD, Negative in SKCM: 5
if (length(prad_neg_skcm_pos) > 0) {
  cat("\nTop genes (negative PRAD, positive SKCM):\n")
  flip_genes <- names(prad_neg_skcm_pos)
  flip_data <- data.frame(
    gene = flip_genes,
    PRAD = mir362_cors[flip_genes, "PRAD"],
    SKCM = mir362_cors[flip_genes, "SKCM"]
  )
  print(head(flip_data[order(flip_data$PRAD - flip_data$SKCM), ], 10))
}
## 
## Top genes (negative PRAD, positive SKCM):
##            gene       PRAD      SKCM
## WDFY1     WDFY1 -0.2951874 0.2822989
## ZC3H7B   ZC3H7B -0.3010475 0.2262190
## FEM1B     FEM1B -0.2538925 0.2261589
## HIPK2     HIPK2 -0.2017527 0.2660746
## ANXA5     ANXA5 -0.2169432 0.2499970
## C5orf22 C5orf22 -0.2435094 0.2197488
## KCNG3     KCNG3 -0.2445354 0.2120882
## HERC1     HERC1 -0.2299996 0.2240853
## USP48     USP48 -0.2009252 0.2485991
## KLRAQ1   KLRAQ1 -0.2211612 0.2246472

Clustering Cancer Types by miR Correlation Patterns

Do cancer types cluster based on how miRNAs correlate with mRNAs? This could reveal tissue-level regulatory patterns.

# Use miR-21 as an example (well-expressed across all cohorts)
mir21 <- grep("miR-21-5p", rownames(miRdata), value = TRUE)[1]
mir21_cors <- compute_mir_matrix(mir21, mRNAdata, miRdata, cancerType)

# Select top 1000 most variable genes for clustering
gene_var <- apply(mir21_cors, 1, var, na.rm = TRUE)
top_genes <- names(sort(gene_var, decreasing = TRUE))[1:1000]
mir21_top <- mir21_cors[top_genes, ]

# Remove cohorts with too many NAs
valid_cohorts <- colSums(!is.na(mir21_top)) > 500
mir21_top <- mir21_top[, valid_cohorts]

# Replace remaining NAs with 0 for clustering
mir21_top[is.na(mir21_top)] <- 0

# Cluster cohorts
pheatmap(t(mir21_top),
         main = "Cancer cohorts clustered by miR-21 correlation patterns\n(top 1000 most variable genes)",
         color = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(100),
         breaks = seq(-0.8, 0.8, length.out = 101),
         show_colnames = FALSE,
         fontsize_row = 8,
         clustering_method = "ward.D2")

Hierarchical clustering of cancer types by miR-21 correlation patterns.

Summary of Findings

This case study analysis reveals several key insights:

  1. Known targets don’t show consistent correlation: Even for well-established direct targets of tumor suppressor miRNAs (let-7a, miR-34a), the correlation patterns are highly variable across cancer types.

  2. Same miR, different behavior: miR-362 has essentially uncorrelated gene correlation profiles in PRAD vs SKCM (r ≈ 0). Many genes show opposite correlations in these two cancers.

  3. Cancer types cluster by miR behavior: When we cluster cancers by their miR-gene correlation patterns, we see tissue-specific groupings, suggesting that the cellular context fundamentally shapes miR-mRNA relationships.

  4. Implications: Researchers should be extremely cautious about:

    • Assuming pan-cancer miR target predictions apply to specific cancer types
    • Using global correlation to infer regulatory relationships
    • Ignoring the tissue context when studying miR function

These findings provide additional support for Kevin’s central thesis that “correlation is not regulation” and that subgroup structure must be considered in correlation analyses.
